texts.full_text: Im working on OpenGL tester program for Android. So i have pretty havy scene with 68 objects, each one has its own class. Im also have Activity, GLView and GLRenderer classes. All objects declared in GLRenderer like so: private final lToe lToe = new lToe(); private final rToe rToe = new rToe(); Is there a good way to load and render these 68 objects while avoiding "card flipping" when loading scenes? Card flipping is the phenomenon where the application crashes and the screen turns gray because it cannot keep up with rendering the objects. A possible solution is to implement asynchronous loading using threading, so that while the OpenGL context is preparing to render one frame, the other thread is loading the next one. This approach can prevent the card flipping effect by allowing the application to render and load simultaneously. You can use the AsyncTask class to implement this functionality. AsyncTask is an abstract class in Android that allows for easy asynchronous execution of background operations. Here is a simplified code snippet to show you how you can achieve this: ```java // Create a class that extends AsyncTask private class LoadObjectsTask extends AsyncTask<Void, Void, Void> { @Override protected Void doInBackground(Void... voids) { // Here you load your objects lToe = new lToe(); rtoe = new rToe(); // continue with the rest of your objects return null; } @Override protected void onPostExecute(void aVoid) { super.onPostExecute(aVoid); // Here you can add your loaded objects to the scene } } // Start the task on a separate thread new LoadObjectsTask().execute(); ``` Note that this is just a basic example and you should adjust the code according to your needs. You should also handle errors and make sure that your application's UI remains responsive. Moreover, you can also look into using a GL surface view's GLSurfaceView.Renderer's onSurfaceCreated() method. This method is called when the rendering surface is created, which is a good place to load your objects. Bu

The book is set during the war, though 1945 seems to be an especially popular year for novelists these days (see also “A Land Remembered” below). Adjusted for inflation, that's above $3.1 million for a married couple, or above $2.3 million for a single person. That's pretty much automatic earnings at that point, and very different from the 2013 tax bracket, where the 7th and final bracket kicks in at $450,000 or $400,000 to get a rate of 39.6 percent. There is no way to compare them in a way that is not arbitrary. It is completely nonsensical to try to summarize how much the rich pay in taxes in a given year by dragging up the top marginal tax rate. I hear it from tHe left, and I hear it from the right, and it's wrong every time. The top rate tells us very little to the point of being nearly useless. texts.preview_tail: since $T_pM_0$ and $T_{i_0(p)}N$ are both isomorphic to $T_pM$. This example illustrates that the condition that $f$ is surjective is necessary, since the pull-back $f^*$ is not injective in general,
texts.full_text: erential forms and submersions I know that the pull-back of a surjective smooth submersion is injective on k-forms on a manifold. That is, if $f:M\rightarrow N$ is surjective and a smooth submersion (for every $p\in M$ the push-forward/differential at p, $f_{*,p}:T_{p}M\rightarrow T_{f(p)}N$ is surjective) then the pull-back $$f^{*}:\Omega^{k}(N)\rightarrow\Omega^{k}(M)$$ is injective. I was wondering on which level the condition that f is surjective is neccesary. Is there a counterexample that illustrate the necessity of this condition? Thank you in advance. A: Suppose $M$ is a manifold and $N=M_0\cup M_1$ is the union of two disjoint copies of $M$, The embedding $i_0:M_0\rightarrow M$ is a smooth submersion, but the pull-back $i_0^*:\Omega^k(N)\rightarrow\Omega^k(M_0)$ is not injective, since $i_0^*(\alpha_0+\alpha_1)=\alpha_0$ for any $\alpha_0\in\Omega^k(M_0)$ and $\alpha_1\in\Omega^k(M_1)$, and $i_0^*(\alpha_1)=0$ for any $\alpha_1\in\Omega^k(M_1)$, so $i_0^(\alpha_0)$, and $\alpha_0+\alpha_1\neq\alpha_0$ in general, for example, $\alpha_1$ could be a non-zero form on $M_1$. This example shows that the condition that $f$ is surjective is necessary, since $i_0$ is a smooth submersion, but the pull-back $i_0^*$ is not injective. To see that $i_0$ is a smooth submersion, note that the differential $i_{0*}:T_pM_0\rightarrow T_{i_0(p)}N$ is an isomorphism, since $T_pM_0$ and $T_{i_0(p)}N$ are both isomorphic to $T_pM$. This example illustrates that the condition that $f$ is surjective is necessary, since the pull-back $f^*$ is not injective in general,

texts.full_text: f missing carotenoid in reducing the fluorescence of single monomeric photosystem II core complexes. The fluorescence of monomeric photosystem II core complexes (mPSIIcc) of the cyanobacterium Thermosynechococcus elongatus, originating from redissolved crystals, is investigated by using single-molecule spectroscopy (SMS) at 1.6 K. The emission spectra of individual mPSIIcc are dominated by sharp zero-phonon lines, showing the existence of different emitters compatible with the F685, F689, and F695 bands reported formerly. The intensity of F695 is reduced in single mPSIIcc as compared to single PSIIcc-dimers (dPSIIcc). Crystal structures show that one of the β-carotene (β-Car) cofactors located at the monomer-monomer interface iinterface, a specific carotenoid molecule called 9',19'-cis α/γ dihydrodicyclic xanthophyll is missing in mPSIIcc. This absence leads to reduced fluorescence intensity of F695 due its role as an efficient quencher for the nearby chlorophylls' excited states at this site, which would otherwise contribute significantly towards overall emission when present within dSPllc but not mlPlIc structures because they lack these particular chromophores altogether; thus resulting in lower signal strength from individual molecules compared with dimeric assemblies where

texts.full_text: ability not only conduct censuses but also define population and ensure that such data is collected following specific rules outlined within Schedule II of DOPR. This legislation ensures a transparent process where municipalities have the autonomy to determine their own populations, yet they are subject to federal standards for census conducting procedures. The schedule specifically dictates certain guidelines including setting dates during April 1st - June 30th annually and submitting final results before September each year in which data is collected. The DOPR provides a three-month period for municipalities to conduct censuses, which begins on April 1 and ends on June 30, and requires the municipalities to set a census date within this period relating to enumeration. texts.full_text: of transmembrane proteins involved in the formation of gap junctions, specific intercellular channels, in invertebrates. Analyses of the Analyses of the innexin genes have revealed an interesting potential regulatory flip-flop genetic mechanism that could control reciprocal gene expression patterns during development andtissue differentiation. This proposed mechanism involves apair or cluster of closely linked but oppositely oriented (head-to-tail) innexingenes. Thekey feature is the presence of overlapping, bidirectional promoters between these paired genes. These promoters can drive transcription in both directions: 1. Bidirectional Transcription Initiation: When one promoter initiatestranscription fromits start site towards the other gene, it simultaneously represses or inhibits the initiation at the opposite promoter due tophysical interference. 2. Reciprocal Expression Patterns: This setup allows for a reciprocal expressionpattern where increased activity of one innexin gene leads to decreased activity of the adjacent gene and vice versa. Such regulation can be crucial during developmental processes, ensuring that specific cell types or tissues express appropriate levels of each protein at different stages. 3. Epigenetic Regulation: The flip-flop mechanism might also involve epigeneticmodificationssuch as DNA methylation or histone modification to further stabilize the expression states and ensure long-term commitment to a particular gene activity pattern.4. Feedback Loops: Additional regulatory elements,including microRNAs (miRNAs) that target one of the transcripts for degradation,can fine-tune this mechanism by creating feedback loops that enhance stabilityand responsiveness to external signals or environmental changes. texts.full_text: (HD, 58%), peritoneal dialysis (PD, 28%), anda combinationof both (14%). The 15D HRQoLscores were calculated for each patient, with higher scores indicating better HRQoL. Before KT, the mean 15D score was 0.80 ± 0.14. After KT, the mean score improved significantly to 0.88 ± 0.11 (p < 0.001). The improvement was seen across all 15 dimensions of the 15Dinstrument, with the most significant gains in dimensions related to physical functioning, such as mobility, vision, and breathing.When comparing the improvement in HRQoL after KT between thedialysis modalities, patients who were on PD before KTshowed the most significant improvement (0.91 ± 0.09 after KT, p < 0.001 compared to beforeKT), followed by patients onHD (0.87 ± 0.11 after KT, p< 0.001), and patientson a combination of HD and PD (0.84 ± 0.10 after KT, p < 0.01).Adherence to medication after KT was also found to play a role in HRQoL. Patients who reported taking their medication as prescribed (88% of thecohort) had a mean 15D score of 0.89 ± 0.10 after KT, compared to 0.83 ± 0.12 for those who reported not always taking their medicationas prescribed (p < 0.05). In conclusion, our study shows that kidney transplantation significantly improves health-related quality of life, with patients whowere on peritoneal dialysis before transplantation benefiting the most. Additionally, adherence to medication after transplantation is crucial formaintaining good HRQoL

texts.full_text: t that a doctor believes should take place in the facility in which they were initially admitted. Gag Rules. H.R. 4250 only prohibits gags on physicians in direct contract with a plan. The majority of doctors, however, contract with plans through medical groups, third party administrators, or other arrangements. Therefore, there are no prohibitions of gag rules for most contracts. External appeals. We are extremely concerned that the external appeals in H.R. 4250 are only advisory -- not binding. The right to an appeal is meaningless if health plans can disregard these decisions. Moreover, patients would be required to pay a fee to participate in an appeals process, up to $100. Consumers should be able to address serious grievances without having to pay. In addition, the plan would be allowed to develop its own definition of medical necessity making it extremely difficult for an enrollee to prevail on appeal. Insufficient enforcement provision. The proposed $250 per day penalties are wholly insufficient for patients who suffer serious harm or even death because of a wrongful action by a health plan. Third, H.R. 4250 contains provisions that have nothing to do with patients' rights and only serve to reduce the likelihood that an acceptable agreement can be reached on this important issue. texts.full_text: reduced in many countries in Africa \[[@CR1]--[@CR3]\]. However, these successes remain limited in geographical coverage while transmission continues in some endemic regions in sub-Saharan Africa despite concerted efforts to reduce or eliminate the disease \[[@CR4], [@CR5]\]. This is partly due to genetic diversity of the main agent *Plasmodium falciparum* which maintains population fitness against targeted interventions such as drugs \[[@CR6], [@CR7]\]. Information on genetic diversity and parasite population trends that could help guide control programmes is lacking in regions with large human populations at risk such as Nigeria. The most recent report on patterns of malaria endemicity in Nigeria continues to show high levels of burden across the country with \~170 million people at risk \[[@CR8]\]. This is despite more than a decadecade of sustained efforts by public health agencies and non-governmental organizations. The complexity arises from various factors including socio-economic conditions, inadequate healthcare infrastructure in rural areas, environmental challenges such as climate change that favor vector breeding sites, poor adherence to preventive measures like the use of insecticide-treated nets (ITNs), intermittent preventative treatment for pregnant women and children under five years old. To address these ongoing issues effectively, there is an urgent need for comprehensive genomic surveillance studies focusing on genetic diversity in Nigeria. Such research would provide critical insights into the evolution of drug resistance markers within parasite populations over time and space across different ecological zones where transmission dynamics vary significantly. This is understandable, since this sector alone accounts for approximately 31% of the 32 billion tons (CO~2~ equivalent/year) of greenhouse gases emitted worldwide ([@bib22]). Meanwhile, the manufacturing sector, contributing 21% of the global greenhouse gas emissions ([@bib21]), is often overlooked. For example, ethylene production via steam cracking releases over 1 ton of CO~2~ per ton of olefin produced ([@bib49]). With a worldwide demand in excess of 150 million tons/year ([@bib19]), ethylene production is one of the largest contributors to greenhouse gas emission in the manufacturing sector. Education and career Born on March 27, 1906, in the Village of Noank, Town of Groton, Connecticut, Anderson received a Bachelor of Arts degree in 1927 from Yale University and a Bachelor of Laws in 1929 from Yale Law School. He entered private practice in New London, Connecticut from 1929 to 1953. He served as a United States Commissioner of the United States District Court for the District of Connecticut in 1936. He was the Public Defender for New London County, Connecticut from 1936 to 1947. He was a member of the Connecticut State Bar Examining Committee from 1936 to 1954. He served in the United States Coast Guard Reserve from 1942 to 1945. He was the State's Attorney for New London County from 1947 to 1953. He was a member of the Connecticut Senate from 1947 to 1949. He served on the Judicial Council of the State of Connecticut from 1952 to 1953. He served as a Judge of the Connecticut Superior Court from 1953 to 1954. He was a Captain in the United States Coast Guard Reserve from 1955 to 1957. Federal judicial service Anderson received his federal judicial appointment on April 26, 1954, when President Dwight D. Eisenhower nominated him to a seat on the United States District Court for the District of Connecticut vacated by Judge Thomas F. Healy. Anderson was confirmed by the United States Senate on May 7, 1954, and received his commission on May 8, 1954. On July 20, 1965, President Lyndon B. johnson nominated Anderson to a seat on the United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit vacated by Judge Charles Edward Clark. Anderson was confirmed by the Senate on August 31, 1965, and received his commission on September 2, 1965. He served as Chief Judge from May 8, 1974

texts.full_text: goal of particle tracking is to determine the interaction between the particle and its environment. The price paid for having a visualization of a particle is a consequent lack of statistics. Here we address the optimal way of extracting diffusion constants from single trajectories for pure Brownian motion. It is shown that the likelihood estimator is much more than the commonly used least squares estimate. Furthermore we investigate the effect of disorder on the distribution of estimated diffusion constants and show that it increases the probability of observing estimates much smaller than the true (average) value.' author: - 'Denis Boyer$^{1}$[^1], David S. texts.full_text: prevalence and effects of adult attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder on work performance in a nationally representative sample of workers. The prevalence and workplace consequences of adult attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) are unknown. An ADHD screen was included in a national household survey (n = 3198, ages 18-44). Clinical re-interviewswere conducted on those scoring above the threshold for probable ADHD (n=257), confirming ADHD diagnoses based on DSM-IV criteria. Results indicated that 4.4% of workers had ADHD, with similar prevalence across occupations and education levels. Workplace consequences included: 1. Lower Job Performance: Adults with ADHD were more likely to report poor performance in their jobs compared to those without ADHD (25.7% vs. 9.6%). 2. Frequent Absenteeism: Workers with ADHD had lower rates of absenteeism, with 38.1% missing at least 15 workdays in the previous year versus 14.6% among those without ADHD. 3. Higher Risk of Job Termination: Those with ADHD were more likely to have been terminated or left a job voluntarily due to performance problems (27.2%) compared to those without ADHD (8.9%). These results suggest that adult ADHD is common in the workplace and can lead to significant workplace consequences, including lower work performance, frequent absenteeism, and increased risk of termination. Employers should consider implementing policies and accommodations to support workers with ADHD and improve their work outcomes. Source: (Ramsay JR, Rostain
